Module 1: Signals

What Electrical Signals from the Body Look Like

Welcome

You’re training to become a doctor. At some point — maybe in neurology, maybe in the ICU, maybe in the OR — you will look at a screen showing electrical signals from a patient’s brain, heart, or muscles.

This tutorial will teach you to read those signals. Not just glance at them — actually understand what you’re seeing.

How This Works

Each module is interactive. You’ll drag sliders, change parameters, and watch signals respond in real time. There are short quiz questions along the way — these are not graded. They’re just checkpoints to make sure the concepts are landing.

Module 8 is graded. That’s where you’ll get real EEG data and find out what’s going on with the patient.

Modules

# Module What You’ll Learn
1 Signals What electrical signals from the body look like and how to describe them
2 Sampling How we capture continuous biological signals with a digital machine
3 Filtering How to clean up a noisy recording to see what matters
4 FFT How to reveal the hidden frequencies in a signal
5 EEG Basics What the EEG measures, where electrodes go, and what the bands mean
6 Artifacts Telling brain activity apart from eye blinks, muscle, and noise
7 Spectrograms Watching brain rhythms change over time
8 The Challenge A real EEG recording. A real clinical question. Your job to figure it out.

What You’ll Need

  • A web browser (you’re using one right now — good start)
  • For Module 8: Python or MATLAB, and a willingness to ask an AI for help writing code

Let’s begin.

Begin Module 1 →

A 28-year-old woman comes to your clinic with episodes of “staring off” lasting 10–15 seconds, happening several times a day. Her partner says she doesn’t respond during these episodes. You order an EEG. The technician hands you a tracing — a wiggly line on a screen.

What are you looking at?

The Basics

Every cell in your body produces tiny electrical signals. Neurons fire. Heart muscle contracts. Skeletal muscle twitches. These are not abstract physics — these are your patient’s physiology, translated into voltage over time.

A signal is just a measurement that changes over time. When we record an EEG, ECG, or EMG, we’re measuring voltage (in microvolts or millivolts) at each moment.

The simplest signal you can imagine is a smooth, repeating wave. It has two properties that matter right now:

  • Amplitude: How tall the wave is. Bigger amplitude = stronger signal.
  • Frequency: How fast the wave repeats. Higher frequency = more cycles per second.

That’s it. Two knobs. Let’s play with them.

Interactive: Build a Signal

Drag the sliders below. Watch what happens to the wave.

//| echo: false

viewof amplitude = Inputs.range([0.5, 10], {
  value: 5,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "Amplitude (μV)"
})

viewof frequency = Inputs.range([0.5, 30], {
  value: 10,
  step: 0.5,
  label: "Frequency (Hz)"
})
//| echo: false

{
  const width = 700;
  const height = 280;
  const margin = {top: 20, right: 20, bottom: 40, left: 55};

  const duration = 2; // seconds
  const sampleRate = 500;
  const nPoints = duration * sampleRate;

  // Generate signal
  const data = Array.from({length: nPoints}, (_, i) => {
    const t = i / sampleRate;
    const y = amplitude * Math.sin(2 * Math.PI * frequency * t);
    return {t, y};
  });

  const svg = d3.create("svg")
    .attr("viewBox", [0, 0, width, height])
    .attr("width", width)
    .attr("height", height)
    .style("background", "#fafafa")
    .style("border-radius", "8px");

  const x = d3.scaleLinear()
    .domain([0, duration])
    .range([margin.left, width - margin.right]);

  const y = d3.scaleLinear()
    .domain([-12, 12])
    .range([height - margin.bottom, margin.top]);

  // Grid lines
  svg.append("g")
    .attr("stroke", "#eee")
    .attr("stroke-width", 0.5)
    .selectAll("line")
    .data(y.ticks(8))
    .join("line")
    .attr("x1", margin.left)
    .attr("x2", width - margin.right)
    .attr("y1", d => y(d))
    .attr("y2", d => y(d));

  // Zero line
  svg.append("line")
    .attr("x1", margin.left)
    .attr("x2", width - margin.right)
    .attr("y1", y(0))
    .attr("y2", y(0))
    .attr("stroke", "#bbb")
    .attr("stroke-width", 1)
    .attr("stroke-dasharray", "4,3");

  // Signal line
  const line = d3.line()
    .x(d => x(d.t))
    .y(d => y(d.y))
    .curve(d3.curveLinear);

  svg.append("path")
    .datum(data)
    .attr("fill", "none")
    .attr("stroke", "#2980b9")
    .attr("stroke-width", 2)
    .attr("d", line);

  // Amplitude annotation
  if (amplitude > 1) {
    const peakT = 1 / (4 * frequency); // first peak
    if (peakT < duration) {
      svg.append("line")
        .attr("x1", x(peakT)).attr("x2", x(peakT))
        .attr("y1", y(0)).attr("y2", y(amplitude))
        .attr("stroke", "#e74c3c")
        .attr("stroke-width", 1.5)
        .attr("stroke-dasharray", "3,2");

      svg.append("text")
        .attr("x", x(peakT) + 6)
        .attr("y", y(amplitude / 2))
        .attr("fill", "#e74c3c")
        .attr("font-size", "12px")
        .attr("font-weight", "600")
        .text(`${amplitude.toFixed(1)} μV`);
    }
  }

  // Period annotation
  if (frequency <= 15) {
    const period = 1 / frequency;
    const startT = 0;
    if (startT + period < duration) {
      svg.append("line")
        .attr("x1", x(startT)).attr("x2", x(startT + period))
        .attr("y1", y(-amplitude - 1.5)).attr("y2", y(-amplitude - 1.5))
        .attr("stroke", "#27ae60")
        .attr("stroke-width", 1.5);

      // Arrow heads
      svg.append("line")
        .attr("x1", x(startT)).attr("x2", x(startT) + 5)
        .attr("y1", y(-amplitude - 1.5)).attr("y2", y(-amplitude - 1.5) - 4)
        .attr("stroke", "#27ae60").attr("stroke-width", 1.5);
      svg.append("line")
        .attr("x1", x(startT)).attr("x2", x(startT) + 5)
        .attr("y1", y(-amplitude - 1.5)).attr("y2", y(-amplitude - 1.5) + 4)
        .attr("stroke", "#27ae60").attr("stroke-width", 1.5);

      svg.append("text")
        .attr("x", x(startT + period / 2))
        .attr("y", y(-amplitude - 1.5) + 16)
        .attr("fill", "#27ae60")
        .attr("font-size", "11px")
        .attr("font-weight", "600")
        .attr("text-anchor", "middle")
        .text(`1 cycle = ${(period * 1000).toFixed(0)} ms`);
    }
  }

  // Axes
  svg.append("g")
    .attr("transform", `translate(0,${height - margin.bottom})`)
    .call(d3.axisBottom(x).ticks(5).tickFormat(d => d + " s"))
    .call(g => g.select(".domain").attr("stroke", "#999"));

  svg.append("g")
    .attr("transform", `translate(${margin.left},0)`)
    .call(d3.axisLeft(y).ticks(6).tickFormat(d => d + " μV"))
    .call(g => g.select(".domain").attr("stroke", "#999"));

  // Title
  svg.append("text")
    .attr("x", width / 2)
    .attr("y", 14)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle")
    .attr("font-size", "13px")
    .attr("fill", "#555")
    .text(`${frequency} Hz signal — ${amplitude.toFixed(1)} μV amplitude`);

  return svg.node();
}

Experiment:

  • Set the frequency to 10 Hz. This is roughly the speed of the alpha rhythm — the dominant EEG wave when you close your eyes.
  • Now crank it to 1 Hz. This is the delta range — slow waves you see in deep sleep.
  • Set amplitude to 2 μV — that’s a weak EEG signal. Now set it to 10 μV — stronger. In real EEG, amplitudes are typically 10–100 μV.

Why Does This Matter Clinically?

Different brain states produce different frequencies and amplitudes:

Brain State Dominant Frequency Typical Amplitude What’s Happening
Deep sleep 1–4 Hz (delta) 100–200 μV Large populations of neurons firing in sync
Relaxed, eyes closed 8–13 Hz (alpha) 30–50 μV Thalamo-cortical idle rhythm
Focused attention 13–30 Hz (beta) 10–20 μV Cortical neurons busy with different tasks
Active thinking >30 Hz (gamma) 5–10 μV Local, fast processing

Notice the pattern: slower rhythms tend to be bigger, faster rhythms tend to be smaller. This isn’t a coincidence — slow rhythms reflect more neurons firing together (synchrony), which sums to a larger voltage at the scalp.

Amplitude tells you how much electrical activity is reaching the electrode. Frequency tells you how fast that activity is oscillating. Together, they are the first two things you assess when you look at any physiological recording — EEG, ECG, or EMG.

It’s Not Just the Brain

The same two properties — amplitude and frequency — describe signals from other parts of the body:

Signal Typical Amplitude Typical Frequency Source
EEG (brain) 10–100 μV 1–100 Hz Cortical pyramidal neurons
ECG (heart) 0.5–3 mV ~1 Hz (60 bpm) Cardiac muscle depolarization
EMG (muscle) 0.1–5 mV 20–500 Hz Motor unit action potentials

The ECG is about 1,000× larger than the EEG. The EMG has much higher frequencies than either. These differences aren’t trivia — they determine what equipment you need, what filters you set, and what artifacts you worry about. (More on artifacts in Module 6.)

Quiz: Check Your Understanding

//| echo: false

// Quiz state
viewof q1answer = Inputs.radio(
  new Map([
    ["A. It means the signal is faster", "a"],
    ["B. It means the voltage is higher — more electrical activity is reaching the electrode", "b"],
    ["C. It means the electrode is closer to the source", "c"],
    ["D. It means the patient is awake", "d"]
  ]),
  {label: "Q1: A signal has a larger amplitude. What does that tell you?"}
)
//| echo: false
q1feedback = {
  if (q1answer === "b") return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-correct">✓ Correct. Amplitude = voltage magnitude. A bigger wave means more electrical activity is being picked up.</div>`;
  if (q1answer === "c") return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-incorrect">✗ Not quite. Electrode distance affects amplitude, but amplitude itself is a property of the signal — it tells you the voltage, not the distance.</div>`;
  if (q1answer === "a") return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-incorrect">✗ That would be frequency, not amplitude. Try again.</div>`;
  if (q1answer === "d") return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-incorrect">✗ Wakefulness can affect amplitude, but amplitude itself just describes how tall the wave is.</div>`;
  return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-hint">Select an answer above.</div>`;
}
//| echo: false

viewof q2answer = Inputs.radio(
  new Map([
    ["A. 0.5 Hz — about one beat every two seconds", "a"],
    ["B. About 1.2 Hz — roughly 70 beats per minute", "b"],
    ["C. 10 Hz — the same as EEG alpha rhythm", "c"],
    ["D. 60 Hz — one beat per second", "d"]
  ]),
  {label: "Q2: A healthy resting heart rate is about 70 beats per minute. What is the frequency of the ECG signal?"}
)
//| echo: false
q2feedback = {
  if (q2answer === "b") return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-correct">✓ Correct. 70 bpm ÷ 60 seconds = ~1.17 Hz. Each cardiac cycle takes about 0.85 seconds.</div>`;
  if (q2answer === "d") return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-incorrect">✗ Careful — 60 beats per minute would be 1 Hz, not 60 Hz. Frequency in Hz = beats per <em>second</em>.</div>`;
  if (q2answer === "a") return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-incorrect">✗ That's too slow — 0.5 Hz would mean 30 bpm (severe bradycardia).</div>`;
  if (q2answer === "c") return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-incorrect">✗ 10 Hz would mean 600 bpm — the patient would not be doing well.</div>`;
  return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-hint">Select an answer above.</div>`;
}
//| echo: false

viewof q3answer = Inputs.radio(
  new Map([
    ["A. More neurons are firing at higher frequency", "a"],
    ["B. More neurons are firing in synchrony, so their voltages add up", "b"],
    ["C. The electrodes are more sensitive to slow signals", "c"],
    ["D. The skull filters out fast signals more than slow ones", "d"]
  ]),
  {label: "Q3: Slow EEG rhythms (like delta during sleep) have much larger amplitude than fast rhythms (like gamma). Why?"}
)
//| echo: false
q3feedback = {
  if (q3answer === "b") return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-correct">✓ Correct. Slow rhythms reflect large populations of neurons firing together. Their individual tiny voltages sum up — like a stadium crowd doing a synchronized wave vs. everyone clapping at different times.</div>`;
  if (q3answer === "d") return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-incorrect">✗ The skull does attenuate signals, but that's not the main reason for the amplitude difference across frequency bands. Think about what the neurons are doing.</div>`;
  if (q3answer === "a") return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-incorrect">✗ It's not about individual neurons firing faster — it's about how many fire <em>together</em>.</div>`;
  if (q3answer === "c") return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-incorrect">✗ The electrodes don't have a frequency preference. The voltage difference comes from the brain itself.</div>`;
  return html`<div class="quiz-feedback-hint">Select an answer above.</div>`;
}

Summary

You now know two things about any physiological signal:

  1. Amplitude — how tall the wave is (measured in μV for EEG, mV for ECG/EMG)
  2. Frequency — how many cycles per second (measured in Hz)

In the next module, we’ll tackle a fundamental question: how does a machine capture a continuously changing biological signal and turn it into numbers a computer can work with?

(Spoiler: it can go wrong in interesting ways.)

← Home Module 2: Sampling →
